corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: We present asynchronous variants of four standard reinforcement learning algorithms and show that parallel actor-learners have a stabilizing effect on training allowing all four methods to successfully train neural network controllers.

context_window.previous: We propose a conceptually simple and lightweight framework for deep reinforce- ment learning that uses asynchronous gradient descent for optimization of deep neural network controllers.
context_window.current: We present asynchronous variants of four standard reinforcement learning algorithms and show that parallel actor-learners have a stabilizing effect on training allowing all four methods to successfully train neural network controllers.
context_window.next: The best performing method, an asynchronous variant of actor-critic, surpasses the current state-of-the-art on the Atari domain while training for half the time on a single multi-core CPU instead of a GPU.

location.sentence: We typically use a convolutional neural network that has one softmax output for the policy π ( a t | s t ; θ ) and one linear output for the value function V ( s t ; θ v ) , with all non-output layers shared.

context_window.previous: Note that while the parameters θ of the policy and θ v of the value function are shown as being separate for generality, we always share some of the parameters in practice.
context_window.current: We typically use a convolutional neural network that has one softmax output for the policy π ( a t | s t ; θ ) and one linear output for the value function V ( s t ; θ v ) , with all non-output layers shared.
context_window.next: We also found that adding the entropy of the policy π to the objective function improved exploration by discouraging premature convergence to suboptimal deterministic poli- cies.

location.sentence: Asynchronous RL Framework We now present multi-threaded asynchronous variants of one-step Sarsa, one-step Q-learning, n-step Q-learning, and advantage actor-critic.

context_window.current: Asynchronous RL Framework We now present multi-threaded asynchronous variants of one-step Sarsa, one-step Q-learning, n-step Q-learning, and advantage actor-critic.
context_window.next: The aim in designing these methods was to find RL algorithms that can train deep neural net- work policies reliably and without large resource require- ments.

location.sentence: In addition to these algorithmic improvements, a number of complementary improvements to the neural network ar- chitecture are possible.

context_window.previous: Yet another, more speculative, direction is to try and combine the recent work on true online tempo- ral difference methods (van Seijen et al., 2015) with non- linear function approximation.
context_window.current: In addition to these algorithmic improvements, a number of complementary improvements to the neural network ar- chitecture are possible.
context_window.next: The dueling architecture of (Wang et al., 2015) has been shown to produce more accurate es- timates of Q-values by including separate streams for the state value and advantage in the network.

location.sentence: These results show that all four methods achieve substantial speedups from using mul- tiple worker threads, with 16 threads leading to at least an order of magnitude speedup.

context_window.previous: Table 2 shows the training speed-up achieved by using increasing numbers of parallel actor-learners av- eraged over seven Atari games.
context_window.current: These results show that all four methods achieve substantial speedups from using mul- tiple worker threads, with 16 threads leading to at least an order of magnitude speedup.
context_window.next: This confirms that our pro- posed framework scales well with the number of parallel workers, making efficient use of resources.

location.sentence: Scalability and Data Efficiency We analyzed the effectiveness of our proposed framework by looking at how the training time and data efficiency changes with the number of parallel actor-learners.

context_window.current: Scalability and Data Efficiency We analyzed the effectiveness of our proposed framework by looking at how the training time and data efficiency changes with the number of parallel actor-learners.
context_window.next: When using multiple workers in parallel and updating a shared model, one would expect that in an ideal case, for a given task and algorithm, the number of training steps to achieve a certain score would remain the same with varying num- bers of workers.

location.sentence: We trained both a feedforward agent with the same architecture as (Mnih et al., 2015; Nair et al., 2015; Van Hasselt et al., 2015) as well as a recurrent agent with an additional 256 LSTM cells after the final hidden layer.

context_window.previous: Specifically, we tuned hyperparameters (learning rate and amount of gradient norm clipping) using a search on six Atari games (Beamrider, Breakout, Pong, Q*bert, Seaquest and Space Invaders) and then fixed all hyperparameters for all 57 games.
context_window.current: We trained both a feedforward agent with the same architecture as (Mnih et al., 2015; Nair et al., 2015; Van Hasselt et al., 2015) as well as a recurrent agent with an additional 256 LSTM cells after the final hidden layer.
context_window.next: We additionally used the final network weights for evaluation to make the results more comparable to the original results Method Training Time Mean Median DQN 8 days on GPU 121.9% 47.5% Gorila 4 days, 100 machines 215.2% 71.3% D-DQN 8 days on GPU 332.9% 110.9% Dueling D-DQN 8 days on GPU 343.8% 117.1% Prioritized DQN 8 days on GPU 463.6% 127.6% A3C, FF 1 day on CPU 344.1% 68.2% A3C, FF 4 days on CPU 496.8% 116.6% A3C, LSTM 4 days on CPU 623.0% 112.6% Table 1.

location.sentence: Deep RL algorithms based on experience replay have achieved unprecedented success in challenging domains such as Atari 2600.

context_window.previous: Aggregating over memory in this way reduces non-stationarity and decorre- lates updates, but at the same time limits the methods to off-policy reinforcement learning algorithms.
context_window.current: Deep RL algorithms based on experience replay have achieved unprecedented success in challenging domains such as Atari 2600.
context_window.next: However, experience replay has several drawbacks: it uses more memory and computation per real interaction; and it requires off-policy learning algorithms that can update from data generated by an older policy.

top_candidates.sentence: We use the Atari domain to compare against state of the art results , as well as to carry out a detailed stability and scalability analysis of the proposed methods.
We now present multi-threaded asynchronous variants of one-step Sarsa, one-step Q-learning, n-step Q-learning, and advantage actor-critic.

While the underlying RL methods are quite different, with actor-critic being an on-policy policy search method and Q-learning being an off-policy value-based method, we use two main ideas to make all four algorithms practical given our design goal.
First, we use asynchronous actor-learners, similarly to the Gorila framework , but instead of using separate machines and a parameter server, we use multiple CPU threads on a single machine.
Second, we make the observation that multiple actors- Algorithm 1 Asynchronous one-step Q-learning -pseudocode for each actor-learner thread. // Assume global shared θ, θ -, and counter T = 0.

Hence, we do not use a replay memory and rely on parallel actors employing different exploration policies to perform the stabilizing role undertaken by experience replay in the DQN training algorithm.
Asynchronous one-step Q-learning: Pseudocode for our variant of Q-learning, which we call Asynchronous onestep Q-learning, is shown in Algorithm 1.
Asynchronous one-step Sarsa: The asynchronous onestep Sarsa algorithm is the same as asynchronous one-step Q-learning as given in Algorithm 1 except that it uses a different target value for Q(s, a).
Asynchronous n-step Q-learning: Pseudocode for our variant of multi-step Q-learning is shown in Supplementary Algorithm S2.

The algorithm is somewhat unusual because it operates in the forward view by explicitly computing nstep returns, as opposed to the more common backward view used by techniques like eligibility traces .
In order to compute a single update, the algorithm first selects actions using its exploration policy for up to t max steps or until a terminal state is reached.
The algorithm then computes gradients for n-step Q-learning updates for each of the state-action pairs encountered since the last update.
Asynchronous advantage actor-critic: The algorithm, which we call asynchronous advantage actor-critic (A3C), maintains a policy π(a t |s t ; θ) and an estimate of the value function V (s t ; θ v ).

The update performed by the algorithm can be seen as ∇ θ log π(a t |s t ; θ )A(s t , a t ; θ, θ v ) where A(s t , a t ; θ, θ v ) is an estimate of the advantage function given by where k can vary from state to state and is upper-bounded by t max .
Optimization: We investigated three different optimization algorithms in our asynchronous framework -SGD with momentum, RMSProp without shared statistics, and RMSProp with shared statistics.
This is one of the most commonly used benchmark environments for RL algorithms.
The pseudocode for the algorithm is presented in Supplementary Algorithm S3.
We propose a conceptually simple and lightweight framework for deep reinforcement learning that uses asynchronous gradient descent for optimization of deep neural network controllers.
We propose a conceptually simple and lightweight framework for deep reinforcement learning that uses asynchronous gradient descent for optimization of deep neural network controllers.

We present asynchronous variants of four standard reinforcement learning algorithms and show that parallel actor-learners have a stabilizing effect on training allowing all four methods to successfully train neural network controllers.

The best performing method, an asynchronous variant of actor-critic, surpasses the current state-of-the-art on the Atari domain while training for half the time on a single multi-core CPU instead of a GPU.
Instead, a variety of solutions have been proposed to stabilize the algorithm .

When applied to a variety of Atari 2600 domains, on many games asynchronous reinforcement learning achieves better results, in far less time than previous GPU-based algorithms, using far less resource than massively distributed approaches.
One example of such an algorithm is Q-learning, which aims to directly approximate the optimal action value function: Q * (s, a) ≈ Q(s, a; θ).
This approach can be viewed as an actor-critic architecture where the policy π is the actor and the baseline b t is the critic .
